questions.prompt: Which feature of the corporate form is most directly responsible for the existence of a public stock market?
questions.explanation: Transferable shares let owners enter and exit without dissolving the firm — that's what a stock market requires. Limited liability and perpetual existence make corporations attractive; transferability is what makes a market possible.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are examples of agency costs in large public corporations?
choices: A CEO pursues an acquisition that grows the firm but lowers shareholder value
Managers refuse a positive-NPV project because it could fail and cost them their jobs
The firm pays a regular dividend
Executive perks (jets, lavish offices) funded out of shareholder profits
questions.explanation: Empire-building, excessive caution, and perks are classic agency costs. Paying a dividend is not — it's actually a tool that mitigates agency cost by removing cash from management's discretion.

questions.prompt: An investor buys 100 shares of Apple from another investor on Nasdaq. Which of the following is true?
choices: No money flows to Apple; this is a secondary-market trade
questions.explanation: Secondary-market trades are between investors. The firm only receives money when it issues new shares (primary market).

questions.prompt: Why does the secondary market matter to a firm even though no money flows to the firm there?
choices: Because liquidity in the secondary market is what makes the primary market work — investors will only buy if they can sell later
Because the secondary-market price funds dividends
Because pension funds only trade in the secondary market
questions.explanation: Liquidity in the secondary market is a precondition for willingness to buy in the primary market. Price discovery is the second benefit — secondary prices guide management decisions.

furtherReading.title: Agency Issues: Shareholders and Corporate Boards
furtherReading.why: Free open-textbook chapter that links the separation of ownership and control to primary/secondary market mechanics — exactly the framing the quiz uses.
